claim: A 2014 review by Goudie found that dust storms contribute up to 50 percent of PM10 in arid regions during storm events and are associated with 5-10 percent increases in respiratory hospitalizations, with effects strongest in children, elderly, and populations with pre-existing respiratory conditions.

source.title: Desert dust and human health disorders

method: Reviewed dust-health literature synthesis, exposure assessment methods, and health-impact estimates across arid regions.
limitations: The 5-10 percent hospitalization increase is a pooled estimate from studies concentrated in high-income arid countries (US Southwest, Australia, Israel) — LMIC arid regions are underrepresented.
Dust-exposure assessment in reviewed studies relied on ground monitors or binary dust-day indicators — satellite AOD-based exposure has not been systematically linked to health outcomes.
The review does not distinguish health effects by dust-source mineralogy or biological contaminant content — toxicological profiles vary by source region.
Confound control for non-dust PM10 sources varies across studies — the pooled estimate may overstate dust-specific effects.

claim: MODIS aerosol optical depth and CALIOP lidar data detect dust plumes at 1-10 km resolution, with the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service providing dust-forecast models at 40 km resolution globally, enabling dust-exposure mapping in regions without ground monitors.

source.title: Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service — Dust Forecasts

method: Reviewed CAMS dust-forecast documentation, MODIS AOD retrieval accuracy in desert environments, and CALIOP lidar dust-detection capabilities.
limitations: MODIS AOD retrieval accuracy is reduced over bright desert surfaces — the bright-surface algorithm has higher uncertainty than over vegetated or ocean surfaces.
CAMS dust-forecast models at 40 km resolution smooth local dust-storm variability — near-source exposure gradients are not captured.
AOD measures column-integrated aerosol, not ground-level concentration — the AOD-to-ground-PM10 relationship depends on boundary-layer height, dust altitude, and deposition rates.
Cloud cover blocks AOD retrieval — dust events co-occurring with cloud may be missed.

claim: Ground air-quality monitoring in LMIC arid regions is extremely sparse — most Sahelian, Central Asian, and Middle Eastern LMIC countries have fewer than one PM10 monitor per million people, making satellite-to-ground exposure validation difficult and health-impact attribution uncertain.

source.title: WHO Ambient Air Quality Database — Monitor Coverage Assessment

method: Reviewed WHO air-quality database for PM10 monitor locations, country coverage, and monitor density in arid LMIC regions.
limitations: The WHO database may not include all monitors — some national or research monitors may be absent.
Monitor count is a snapshot — networks may have expanded or contracted since the assessment.
Fewer than one monitor per million people means most districts have zero monitors — satellite exposure estimates cannot be validated at sub-national scale.
PM10 measurement methods vary across monitors — some use reference methods while others use less accurate techniques.
